agent_approach_summary: The agent located `windowsReleases` and `detectKBsFromKernelVersion`, recognized that the fix was a data update to add missing Windows KB/revision mappings, and attempted to manually append newer entries for Windows 10/11 and Server 2022. It then got stuck trying to determine the exact KB/revision pairs and made inconsistent guesses before timing out without a final patch.
contamination_evidence: The F2P tests require exact hard-coded KB/revision facts that are not given in the issue text: e.g. Windows 10 19045 must yield unapplied KBs `5023696, 5023773, 5025221, 5025297, 5026361, 5026435, 5027215, 5027293`; Windows 11 22621 must include `5022913, 5023706, 5023778, 5025239, 5025305, 5026372, 5026446, 5027231, 5027303`; Server 2022 20348 must map `1547 -> 5022842`, then `1607 -> 5023705`, `1668 -> 5025230`, `1726 -> 5026370`, `1787 -> 5027225`, and treat `20348.9999` as fully patched. None of these concrete KBs, revisions, or even build numbers appear in the problem statement. The repository only contains comments linking to external Microsoft update-history pages, so the authoritative data needed by the tests is external and not derivable from the prompt alone.
reasoning: This looks under-specified rather than merely hard. The agent clearly understood the nature of the bug: update the static Windows KB/revision mapping so the detector can report newer monthly updates and recognize high revisions as patched. It found the correct table and function, verified that the relevant KB range was absent, and started editing the data. The failure came when it needed the exact Windows release-history facts. The tests are behavioral rather than coupled to internal helper names, so this is not approach-lock or test-coupling; however, they assert very specific KB/revision sequences that the issue never enumerates. In an offline benchmark, those constants are effectively undiscoverable unless already known or leaked by the tests/gold patch. The trajectory supports this: the agent began guessing mappings and mixing wrong KBs/revisions across tracks, which is exactly what happens when the task depends on external release-history data absent from the spec. So the task is likely contaminated by undiscoverable requirements.
